Client 2: And I’m Joseph. I’m still self-employed, so I’m thinking about the cash flow side—like what changes when I stop working.
Advisor: That makes sense. We’ll keep it practical and also map it to the longer-term plan. Let me start with the big picture: what are you seeing for total household gross income per year right now?
Client 1: The household gross income is 131950 annually.
Advisor: Got it—thank you. And just so I’m not mixing sources, Susan, what’s your portion versus Joseph’s, roughly?
Client 1: Mine is $71,600 a year.

Client 2: We’re around five-ish. Like $5,300 most months.
Advisor: Okay—so roughly in that range. Susan, do you have the tighter number you track?
Client 1: Yes—total monthly expenses are 5350.
Advisor: Great, thank you. Now, debt: any monthly debt payments—mortgage, car loans, credit cards you’re carrying month to month?
Client 2: No, we don’t have any payments like that.

Client 1: Investable assets total 815850.
Advisor: Perfect—thank you. Now, protection planning: Susan, you mentioned long-term care. Do you currently have a long-term care policy in place?
Client 1: Yes, I do. It’s long-term care.

Client 2: And a small private markets thing—on a retirement platform—$6,250.

Advisor: Perfect. Now on taxes—when you think about your marginal bracket, where do you usually land? I don’t need a perfect calculation, just the band you plan around.
Client 2: I always think of us as, like, mid-thirties—
Client 1: Yeah, to be precise, we plan around 35%.
Advisor: Got it—thank you. And last admin-ish item: how do you want to think about our relationship here—are you engaging me as an RIA, a registered investment advisor, versus, say, a brokerage arrangement?
Client 1: Yes—RIA (registered investment advisor).

Client 2: I always think of us as, like, mid-thirties—
Client 1: Yeah, to be precise, we plan around 35%.
Advisor: Got it—thank you. And last admin-ish item: how do you want to think about our relationship here—are you engaging me as an RIA, a registered investment advisor, versus, say, a brokerage arrangement?
Client 1: Yes—RIA (registered investment advisor).
Advisor: Perfect. While I’ve got you—Susan, earlier you mentioned the long-term care policy with the $500 monthly premium and $1.04M coverage through 2052-11-28. Still the only protection policy we’re tracking right now?

Advisor: Excellent—thank you. That gives me a clean liabilities section: no mortgage, no other debt, and the house at that value.
Advisor: Okay—thanks, Susan. That all hangs together. Let’s pivot to a couple of household basics that drive the tax and planning assumptions.
Advisor: First, just to confirm your marital status—Susan and Joseph, are you married?
Client 1: Yes, we’re married.
Advisor: Great. And country for planning purposes—are you based in the US?

Advisor: Hi Susan, hi Joseph—nice to meet you both. I’m glad you came in. Before we get into numbers, what prompted you to reach out now?
Client 1: Thanks. I’m Susan. We’re trying to get really clear on retirement timing and, honestly, long-term care planning—so we’re not guessing later.
Client 2: And I’m Joseph. I’m still self-employed, so I’m thinking about the cash flow side—like what changes when I stop working.

Client 2: And I’m Joseph. I’m still self-employed, so I’m thinking about the cash flow side—like what changes when I stop working.

Client 1: The household gross income is 131950 annually.
Advisor: Got it—thank you. And just so I’m not mixing sources, Susan, what’s your portion versus Joseph’s, roughly?
Client 1: Mine is $71,600 a year.

Advisor: And how long is that coverage set to run—do you have an end date on it?
Client 1: Covered until 2052-11-28.
Advisor: Great—thank you. And just to close the loop on liabilities: other than normal monthly bills, you’re saying no loans outstanding at all right now, correct?
Client 2: Correct—no loans outstanding.
Advisor: Okay, thanks—assets and the long-term care policy are clear. I want to tighten up the debt picture for the household, just so we’re not missing anything.

Advisor: Hi Susan, hi Joseph—nice to meet you both. I’m glad you came in. Before we get into numbers, what prompted you to reach out now?
Client 1: Thanks. I’m Susan. We’re trying to get really clear on retirement timing and, honestly, long-term care planning—so we’re not guessing later.
Client 2: And I’m Joseph. I’m still self-employed, so I’m thinking about the cash flow side—like what changes when I stop working.
Advisor: That makes sense. We’ll keep it practical and also map it to the longer-term plan. Let me start with the big picture: what are you seeing for total household gross income per year right now?
Client 1: The household gross income is 131950 annually.

Advisor: Makes sense. Now, zooming out—risk. Earlier you were talking about staying invested and not overreacting. If you had to pick a simple label for how you want the portfolio positioned, where do you land?
Client 2: I’m okay with some ups and downs, but I don’t want anything crazy—
Client 1: Growth. Risk tolerance is growth.
Advisor: Great—thank you. And Joseph, you’re comfortable with that as long as we tie it back to the spending plan and the retirement timing?
Client 2: Yeah, exactly. If you show me what it means for monthly spending, I’m fine with growth.

Advisor: Got it—thank you. And just so I’m not mixing sources, Susan, what’s your portion versus Joseph’s, roughly?
Client 1: Mine is $71,600 a year.
Client 2: And mine is $60,350—give or take, but that’s the number we use.
Advisor: Perfect. And the income itself—what’s it coming from mostly right now? W-2, business, portfolio income…?
Client 1: It’s mostly interest and dividends at this point, paid monthly.

Client 2: Yes, spouse/partner.
Advisor: Great. And I know you mentioned you’re self-employed—what would you call your work in plain English? Like, what bucket does it fall into day-to-day?
Client 2: It’s operations. I basically keep the wheels turning—vendors, scheduling, that kind of stuff.
Advisor: Got it. When you say operations, is that mostly for your own business, or are you doing ops work for someone else as a contractor?
Client 2: For my own thing—self-employed, but yeah, it’s operations work.

Advisor: Okay—thanks. Before we wrap today, I want to make sure I’ve got your details right, Joseph, since a lot of the cash-flow hinges on you. Can we talk through your role and income for a minute?
Client 2: Yeah, sure.
Advisor: First, just confirming household roles: Susan is the primary client, and Joseph—you’re the spouse/partner in the plan, right?
Client 2: Yes, spouse/partner.
Advisor: Great. And I know you mentioned you’re self-employed—what would you call your work in plain English? Like, what bucket does it fall into day-to-day?
